01 — Client Project

The Latest Scoop

Brand Narrative Realignment

Context

Canadian lifestyle and home retailer in leadership transition, seeking to realign brand narrative for an evolving audience.

Challenge

Refresh the brand story, retain legacy language, and support a widening audience without alienating the existing customer base.

Constraints

Retention of legacy language. Existing agency work required selective integration due to tone misalignment. Client required intentional humor where appropriate.

Deliverables

  • Revised Brand Story — multiple directions
  • Updated Brand Documentation

Original

"A lifestyle concept store offering a curated selection of pretty things for you and your home: fashion, home decor, accessories, furniture, stationery, giftware and much more."

Legacy Copy

"Our Purpose is to inspire our people and our customers to embrace their individuality through genuine interactions."

Legacy Copy

Revised

"All the pretty things of Paris, Rome, and London. All our favorite designs from New York to Madrid, from Los Angeles to Bali. All the beauty and meaning for you and your home.

Beginning in 2004 as a series of pop-ups, The Latest Scoop has grown to eight permanent locations based in our favorite Vancouver, Victoria and Toronto neighborhoods.

As a lifestyle concept store, our passion is to bring you the freshest looks, from our travels, week to week. Carefully curated, our stores are as seasonal and inspiring as they are beautiful."

Sam Broadway

02 — Spec Project

Inner Form Studio

Narrative + Positioning

Overview

A narrative studio reconnecting organizations to purpose through language. When it comes to language, we believe function follows form.

Voice

Compassionate Measured Grounded Accessible

Works With

Organizations in cultural transition. Founders seeking greater meaning. Brands with a public conscience.

Does Not Work With

Companies who pursue profit at the expense of people.

Services

  • Brand Narrative / Positioning Document
  • Narrative Style Guide
  • Key Web Copy
  • Messaging Framework
  • Stewardship Outline & Agreement

03 — Spec Project

Single Form Studio

Object Design + Product Copy

Studio Overview

An object design studio that employs repetition and constraint to cultivate purpose.

Core Idea

We repeat the simple forms of a sphere and a cylinder. By adhering to this constraint, we deepen our attention to emerging function.

Voice

Restrained Considered Elegant

Flagship Product

An incense burner that catches ash and stores it long term, reducing mess and frequency of cleaning. Ash falls onto the curved surface of a sphere, guided into the hidden well below, and collects over time.

Key Lines

Because the best part is beginning again.

From silence a form, from stillness a function.

Designed with your attention in mind.

04 — Spec Project

The Well on Main

Brand · Copy · Design System · Digital

Overview

A comprehensive spec project for a contemplative yoga and meditation studio in Richmond, VA's Fan District. Positioned against the shallowness of mainstream wellness — grounded in textual lineage, pedagogy, and the serious transmission of Eastern contemplative practice.

Voice

Warm, Assuring Composed, Staid Pedagogical Curious, Relevant Considered, Precise

Deliverables

  • Brand Narrative + Positioning
  • Mission Statement
  • Weekly Schedule
  • Event Description
  • Homepage Copy
  • About Page Copy
  • Newsletter — The Sip, Issue 01
  • Welcome Email
  • Figma — Desktop + Mobile Homepage
  • Figma — About Page, Desktop + Mobile
  • Figma — Style Guide
  • Social Mockups — Two Instagram Posts
  • Newsletter Mockup — Substack
  • Welcome Email Mockup

Case Study

The conception of this project was as personal as it was professional. Intended to demonstrate the execution of a comprehensive set of deliverables, The Well on Main is also a reaction to my perception, as a practitioner of yoga and meditation for the past ten years, of the shallowness typical to many wellness spaces and brands. I sought to design a meditation and yoga studio, operating as a business, that I would want to be involved with, that I would be grateful to have in my community — one that grounds its services in the philosophical teachings and textual lineage of Eastern contemplative traditions, that encourages deeper engagement while remaining accessible to everyone, and incorporates the transference of knowledge as a vital component of its community model.

While it was important that The Well on Main feel transportable, I primarily kept Richmond, Virginia's The Fan District neighborhood in mind both for its cultural literacy and receptiveness. The name, however, evolved even prior to this selection; "Broadway Sangha," which contained an admittedly double-meaning by sharing my surname, became "First Ave Sangha." Preferring accessibility over exclusivity, I opted for "The Well," signaling depth metaphorically, while utilizing "on Main" both to retain geographical universality as well as to infer an institutional air.

Commitment to a deeply informed practice is what sets The Well apart from its competitors. This is reflected in its programming, which features free meditations, provocative weekly discussions, and ritualized Sunday lectures set in a disciplined, unshifting schedule that is itself a gentle, yet strong selling point. It is also foundational to its aesthetic profile.

The selection of Cormorant as the homepage's sole typeface conveys the literary seriousness, restraint, and consistency established by The Well's belief system. Similar considerations informed the choice of background color, #e8e0d4; a brighter cream was initially chosen, but I felt this was too generic and energetic for The Well, opting instead for something just as warm but with more depth and age. Visitors ought to feel drawn into the experience, sensing a gravity to their selection of and participation in the services provided.

The Well's core values are further communicated both in the background image as well as the user's interaction with it. I first considered showcasing the space a client might expect to encounter; however, finding an acceptable image was challenging, and I decided displaying an empty studio articulated very little about the brand. The photo of a woman sitting on a meditation cushion in an existing studio illuminated by a warm, ambient light not only had the appropriate tone, it also suggested a relationship The Well aspires to create; she appears to be engaged with a student beyond the frame, making eye contact as if giving instruction. Future photograph selection would do well to showcase this element of pedagogy while expanding to include additional members of an apparently growing community.

The overlaying of color-backgrounded text boxes on the image suggests a parallax scroll function. As the user moves down the page, the image reveals itself by remaining static until it eventually terminates altogether; somewhat paradoxically, this creates the sense that the user is moving deeper into the page, creating a small but nevertheless interactive relationship to the brand's identity.

Interactivity itself became a useful constraint for copy decisions. In the hero line, "begins" is italicized rather than "within" in order to subtly communicate the notion that the transformative journey ultimately moves outward, from within one individual's mind towards a community of practitioners aiding one another in this process. The choice of the word "juicy" in the description of The Well's Thursday night programming is a moment of deliberate character-breaking that accentuates this commitment to community by asserting itself as contemporary, relevant, and willingly provocative. Similarly, yet more typical of the brand's voice, the line "Our schedule doesn't change, so yours never has to," distills brand value from logistical decisions.

With this reasoning, the voice, style, and name of the brand's newsletter were also developed. Instead of a mere reportage of past and upcoming events, The Sip is written in an essayistic manner as if from the perspective of a well-informed, engaged participant; an event did not simply occur, an understanding was arrived at. Readers of The Sip don't learn about what happened, they encounter what event attendees encountered, albeit in a shorter form: "If we reach a thousand times a day, is this not a thousand moments to examine why?" The newsletter's CTA — "You can sit with us even while you're away" — is an invitation to drink from the same well. The page closes with the conversion of the hero line's promise into a gentle command: "Begin at The Well."

Each decision made in the development of The Well on Main — from the selection of a single typeface to the italicization of a single word — was made in conscious relationship to every other design choice. The coherence between them is a product of a successive informing of one by another and functions as a deliverable in and of itself. The style guide as such, rather than prescribing, documents an already functioning design ecosystem.

Event Description — Example

Zen and the Art of Scrolling

We all know the feeling: the phone is in our hand, the app is open — we don't even remember wanting to pick up the phone, let alone reaching for it. We know we've lost time, that our focus has been hijacked, that our responsibilities and dreams suffer from this inattentiveness. How did something we've come to hate, something we know to be bad for us, become so automatic?

Our willpower is not to blame for a response as highly engineered as the environment that's caused it. And while the yogic and meditative traditions did not predict the arrival of the iPhone, they have always anticipated the wandering mind — what Patanjali called chitta vritti.

In this talk we'll examine what the ancient texts have to offer our experiences of the attention economy and find our way not out of modern life, but back into it, together. All phones are welcome.

Newsletter — Sample

The Sip — Issue 01

Notes from Zen & the Art of Scrolling

Is it possible that there is more to this automatic habit? When we reach for our phones, is it only the object, the world it contains, we are reaching for?

We have come to know this as addiction, one we love guiltily and hate bitterly. It is a tool we use for a job we didn't decide upon. Sometimes we need, even deserve, a little doom, an excuse to do next to nothing. Constantly inundated is the new being bored. But the scroll doesn't stop once we've lost time for it. It grabs hold of us when we want most to be free of it.

We think because it's so new there is nothing we can do about it. But the smart phone is just the latest container for a forever problem, one with an equally infinite solution.

The Yoga Sutras describe this very problem as one foundational to the practice itself. Chitta vritti — the wandering mind, the restless, grasping mind. This is not a description of a flaw, but a description of its very nature. Our mind does not exist without this quality, this ability to become distracted. And not simply to become distracted, but to generate more and more distractedness.

At the time the Sutras were written, the container for the mind's distraction was quite different. As populations grew, cities proliferated, marketplaces spawned — new forms on which to place one's evermore fractured attention, more commerce, more things, more noise. The mind sought refuge in its own external reflection.

Knowing this, it is somewhat less surprising that even novel reading was, in the 19th Century, considered by some to be nothing more than an inebriating, debilitating addiction. Of course, we think we know better now.

Today, we look at our phones and we see a cause. If it weren't for this, I would be focused, I wouldn't lose time, I wouldn't get off track. But this device is just taking advantage of our own inherent restlessness. We look for it — at times desperately — and it finds us. But we were already wandering long before it arrived.

This new thing is not the source of our problem but its mirror. No tool has ever before so effectively captured the ceaseless movements of the mind. With this understanding, we can begin to view this device with compassion; rather than the thief of our desire, it is its most accurate map.

Each time we reach for it, what are we really reaching for? Connection? Stimulation? Validation? Joy? Escape? If we reach a thousand times a day, is this not a thousand moments to examine why?

There is immense gratitude to be discovered in such small moments, if we can make them less automatic. By honoring the pull as proportional to the need it conceals, by asking ourselves what this need really is, we expand this moment and offer an opportunity for return.
